message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 13
Recent Green phase: Switched premium calc from a multiplicative (base×1.1) model to an ADDITIVE-modifier model. Item interface now has optional material/enchantment/cursed. New helpers: mainItemBase(item)=BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]; curseSurcharge(items)=sum over non-component cursed items of base×0.5. quotePremium now = ceil(policyBase + curseSurcharge + firstInsurance(policyBase×0.1) + PROCESSING_FEE). Constants FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE=0.1, CURSE_RATE=0.5 replaced the old numerator/denominator pair.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Watch for: float-precision risk now that rates are decimals (0.1, 0.5) — but all current tests pass, so do not change behavior; only restructure if metrics improve. Evaluate naming first, then Four Rules of Simple Design, compute APP mass + McCabe + cognitive before/after. Note the upcoming feature space (high-enchantment surcharge, loyalty/follow-up discounts) — but DO NOT implement ahead; only refactor existing code.

Run tests with `pnpm test`.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
PRE-measurement baseline established.

## Step 0: PRE-Measurement (Smells + Cognitive)

ESLint: **0 smells, 0 cognitive-complexity warnings**. The surface is already clean. 13/13 tests passing.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

Walking each function name against its current purpose:

- `componentBasePremium(count)` — returns the premium for a block of N alike components. Name fits.
- `isComponent(item)` — predicate for component types. Clear.
- `countByType(items)` — builds a type→count map. Clear.
- `componentTotal(items)` — sum of component block premiums. Clear.
- `basePremium(items)` — main items + components. Clear.
- `mainItemBase(item)` — base premium for one main item. Clear.
- `curseSurcharge(items)` — sum of curse surcharges. Clear.
- `quotePremium(items)` — final ceil'd premium. Clear.
- `runScenario(scenario)` — maps steps to results. Clear.

All names reveal intent. No rename needed.

## Step 2: PRE-Measurement (APP Mass)

Computing mass for the functions touched/related to the Green change. The most structurally interesting are `basePremium`, `mainItemBase`, `curseSurcharge`, `quotePremium`.

**`mainItemBase`** — `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]`: 1 binding (item) + 1 invocation (index access) ≈ mass 3.

**`curseSurcharge`** — filter+reduce with `!isComponent(item) && item.cursed`: bindings(items, item ×2, sum) ~4, invocations(filter, isComponent, reduce, mainItemBase, ×) ~10, conditionals(`&&`) 4, loops(filter+reduce) 10 → mass ≈ 28.

**`quotePremium`**:
```
const policyBase = basePremium(items);        // assignment 6, invocation 2, binding 1
const firstInsurance = policyBase * FIRST...; // assignment 6, invocation 2 (×), bindings 2
const itemSurcharges = curseSurcharge(items); // assignment 6, invocation 2, binding 1
return Math.ceil(policyBase + ... + ...);     // invocations: ceil, +×3 = 8; bindings 4
```
Constants: 0. Bindings ≈ 8. Invocations ≈ 14. Assignments 3×6 = 18. Mass ≈ 8 + 28 + 18 = **54**.

## Step 3: PRE-Measurement (McCabe)

- `componentBasePremium`: 1 + ternary 1 = **2**
- `isComponent`: **1**
- `countByType`: 1 + `??` 1 = **2**
- `componentTotal`: **1**
- `basePremium`: 1 + arrow `!isComponent` 0 branches = **1**
- `mainItemBase`: **1**
- `curseSurcharge`: 1 + `&&` 1 = **2**
- `quotePremium`: **1** (no branches)
- `runScenario`: **1**

Worst McCabe is 2 (three functions tied). All extremely low — well below any threshold.

## Assessment

Every metric is at or near its floor:
- ESLint smells: 0
- Cognitive complexity: no function flagged (all trivial)
- Max McCabe: 2
- Naming: all reveal intent
- Duplication (Rule 3): `quotePremium` uses three intermediate `const` assignments that read clearly; no duplicated knowledge across functions.

The one place with a defensible micro-refactoring is `quotePremium`: the three named intermediates each add assignment mass (6 apiece). But those names (`policyBase`, `firstInsurance`, `itemSurcharges`) are exactly the kind of intent-revealing locals Rule 2 favors — and the upcoming features (loyalty discount, follow-up discount, high-enchantment surcharge) will plug into precisely this assembly point. Collapsing them to lower APP mass would trade clarity and future-extensibility for a meaningless number, violating "Rule 2 trumps APP."

Let me verify there's no genuine duplication I'm missing in the filter predicates.

There's a small conceptual duplication: `!isComponent(item)` appears in both `basePremium` (line 56) and `curseSurcharge` (line 65) — both functions operate on "main items" (non-components). This is Rule 3 (No Duplication). Extracting an `isMainItem` predicate would name that concept and DRY up the two filters.

Let me evaluate the trade. `isMainItem(item) = !isComponent(item)` would:
- Remove the duplicated negation knowledge ("main item = not a component") — currently expressed twice.
- Add one tiny named function that reveals intent (Rule 2 benefit too).
- McCabe of new function: 1. APP mass: ~3. No new branches.
- The call sites become `.filter(isMainItem)` instead of `.filter((item) => !isComponent(item))` — fewer elements at each call site (Rule 4).

This is a legitimate improvement on Rules 2, 3, and 4 simultaneously, with no metric regression. Let me apply this single change.

input.new_string:
const isComponent = (item: Item): boolean => COMPONENT_TYPES.has(item.type);

const isMainItem = (item: Item): boolean => !isComponent(item);

content.text:
## Step 6: POST-Measurement (APP + McCabe)

**New `isMainItem`**: `!isComponent(item)` — binding (item) 1, invocation (isComponent) 2, `!` is not a branch → McCabe **1**, mass ~3.

**`basePremium`**: filter callback went from `(item) => !isComponent(item)` to bare `isMainItem`. Removed one binding + one invocation + the `!` operator from this function → APP mass dropped (~−5), McCabe unchanged at **1**.

**`curseSurcharge`**: `!isComponent(item)` → `isMainItem(item)`. Replaced the `!` + `isComponent` call with a single `isMainItem` call — neutral on mass, McCabe still **2** (the `&&` remains).

The negation knowledge "main item = not a component" now lives in exactly one place.

## Refactoring Complete

**Refactoring Applied**: Extracted `isMainItem(item) = !isComponent(item)` predicate, removing duplicated negation logic from `basePremium` and `curseSurcharge` (Rule 3: No Duplication; also Rules 2 and 4 at call sites).

**Pre/Post Deltas**:
- ESLint smells: 0 → 0 (no new smells, stayed clean)
- Cognitive complexity: no function flagged before or after
- APP mass (`basePremium`): ~−5 (removed inline negation + call); new `isMainItem` mass ~3
- McCabe: all functions unchanged (max remains 2); new `isMainItem` = 1
- Duplication: `!isComponent` knowledge expressed twice → once

**Tests**: 13/13 passing.

Note for upcoming features: the assembly point in `quotePremium` (`policyBase + itemSurcharges + firstInsurance + PROCESSING_FEE`) is where the high-enchantment surcharge and loyalty/follow-up discounts will plug in. I deliberately kept its three named intermediates rather than collapsing them for lower APP mass — Rule 2 (reveal intent) trumps APP, and those names map directly to the discount/surcharge slots the next tests will fill. The float-precision risk from decimal rates (0.1, 0.5) was left untouched per instructions since all tests pass and `Math.ceil` currently absorbs it.

Implementation file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_01-30-14_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
